coredata.dc:title: Unlocking dual purposes of microwave radiation for ultrafast synthesis of carbon dots and deep eutectic solvent: Fluorescence switch-off nanosensor integrated with air-assisted liquid–liquid microextraction for flavoxate analysis in plasma

coredata.dc:creator: Abd-AlGhafar, Walaa Nabil
Shabana, Rasha Abo
El-Shaheny, Rania
Tolba, Manar M.
coredata.dc:description: In this contribution, we report an ultrafast, facile, safe, and economical smart dual engaging of microwave radiation for synthesizing highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and deep eutectic solvent (DES) for flavoxate HCl (FLV) analysis and extraction, respectively. A fluorescent nanosensor was derived from expired gabapentin and ammonium persulfate (GABA-CDs) via a 3-min microwave-induced polymerization through a one-stone-two-birds strategy. The prepared GABA-CDs exhibit blue fluorescence at 475 nm when excited at 370 nm with an average particle size of 3.6 nm. The anticholinergic drug FLV dramatically quenched the GABA-CDs fluorescence by a synergistic combination of inner filter effect and static mechanism. The new GABA-CDs nanoprobe has been optimized by a design of experiment. The method revealed good linearity for FLV over the concentration range of 0.3–24.0 µg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.094 µg mL−1. A novel hydrophobic DES was synthesized for the first time via a 60 sec microwave irradiation of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide and glycerol in a molar ratio of (1:4) for efficient air-assisted liquid–liquid microextraction of FLV from plasma with recovery % of 91.0 to 104.5 %. The developed approach paves the way for new avenues in boosting microwave role in analytical chemistry labs from probe to extractant synthesis and offering an efficient and fast alternative to the traditional synthesis procedures. The designed sensing platform granted excellent biocompatibility and anti-interference capability for FLV in the presence of many different interferents. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied to estimate FLV in tablets and human plasma, hinting potential applications in FLV monitoring. The approach is green, confirmed by ComplexGAPI, AGREE score of 0.7, and BAGI score of 75.

head.ce:title: Unlocking dual purposes of microwave radiation for ultrafast synthesis of carbon dots and deep eutectic solvent: Fluorescence switch-off nanosensor integrated with air-assisted liquid–liquid microextraction for flavoxate analysis in plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this contribution, we report an ultrafast, facile, safe, and economical smart dual engaging of microwave radiation for synthesizing highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and deep eutectic solvent (DES) for flavoxate HCl (FLV) analysis and extraction, respectively. A fluorescent nanosensor was derived from expired gabapentin and ammonium persulfate (GABA-CDs) via a 3-min microwave-induced polymerization through a one-stone-two-birds strategy. The prepared GABA-CDs exhibit blue fluorescence at 475 nm when excited at 370 nm with an average particle size of 3.6 nm. The anticholinergic drug FLV dramatically quenched the GABA-CDs fluorescence by a synergistic combination of inner filter effect and static mechanism. The new GABA-CDs nanoprobe has been optimized by a design of experiment. The method revealed good linearity for FLV over the concentration range of 0.3–24.0 µg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.094 µg mL−1. A novel hydrophobic DES was synthesized for the first time via a 60 sec microwave irradiation of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide and glycerol in a molar ratio of (1:4) for efficient air-assisted liquid–liquid microextraction of FLV from plasma with recovery % of 91.0 to 104.5 %. The developed approach paves the way for new avenues in boosting microwave role in analytical chemistry labs from probe to extractant synthesis and offering an efficient and fast alternative to the traditional synthesis procedures. The designed sensing platform granted excellent biocompatibility and anti-interference capability for FLV in the presence of many different interferents. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied to estimate FLV in tablets and human plasma, hinting potential applications in FLV monitoring. The approach is green, confirmed by ComplexGAPI, AGREE score of 0.7, and BAGI score of 75.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Flavoxate hydrochloride (FLV), piperidinylethyl 3-methyl-4-oxo-2-phenyl-4H-chromene-8-carboxylate hydrochloride [1], is a flavonoid-like drug with a strong smooth muscle relaxing effect [2]. It works directly on the genitourinary system’s muscle, stopping spasms in the urethra and bladder. It has anticholinergic and antimuscarinic effects, and it is used for the relief of dysuria, urinary frequency, and incontinence [3]. Besides, FLV is used for urinary urgency following pelvic radiotherapy. However, overdose may cause drowsiness, nervousness, hyperpyrexia, wheezing, confusion, and blurred vision [3]. Clinical monitoring of FLV levels in plasma can reduce such side effects by a suitable drug dosage guide and assure treatment efficacy and the patient’s safety. Therefore, developing an accurate and rapid FLV assay in plasma is of paramount importance.

Up to now, a number of methods have been reported for FLV assay, employing high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–10], electrochemical procedures [11–14], few spectrophotometry [8,15], and one spectrofluorimetric method [16]. United States Pharmacopeia [17] states HPLC procedure, while British Pharmacopoeia [1] recommends non-aqueous potentiometric titration with 0.1 M perchloric acid and spectrophotometry for its assay in pure form and tablets, respectively. Limited approaches have been reported for the analysis of FLV in biological fluids, such as HPLC [9,10] and electrochemical [14] approaches. However, the reported HPLC procedures [9,10] are costly, especially with tandem mass detector [10]. Besides, they are time-consuming, complex, not eco-friendly by using excessively hazardous organic solvents, and not affordable in resource-limited laboratories. Additionally, the electrochemical analysis suffered from the complexity and expensive assembly of electro-chemosensors [11–14]. Yet, there is no published method touching on the fluorescent probe for the cited drug. Despite this technique offering various merits, including simplicity, real-time detection, high selectivity and sensitivity, unnecessity of laborious sample preparation or specialized knowledge, cost-efficiency, and wide accessibility of the instrument in quality control laboratories. Given these merits, FLV can benefit from the recently emerged nanomaterial-based probes that permitted the estimation of numerous non-fluorescent compounds. These attributes motivated the authors to initiate the present study aiming to design a new integrated green strategy that works by greening different sides of the analytical procedure. The invented approach depends on boosting the power of microwave radiation to intensify the different stages of the proposed method from the synthesis of the analytical probe to the synthesis of deep eutectic solvent (DES) for sample pretreatment. The designed method is considered the first spectrofluorimetric one to determine FLV in plasma.

Microwave-assisted synthesis (MWAS) is a non-classical technique that progressively gained popularity and remarkably decreases reaction times. At present, MWAS is considered one of the most efficient, affordable, inexpensive, and fast routes to prepare fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) due to its ease of use, diminishing gas and energy consumption, contactless heat transfer to the reactants, and no multiple instruments or multisteps [18]. MWAS together with greener substrates and reaction media dramatically minimize reaction waste and time. These substantial advantages motivated the authors to design an optimized complemented approach for FLV analysis based on MWAS of CDs as a sensitive nanoprobe, beside ultrafast MWAS of a novel DES for FLV extraction from biological samples prior to analysis. Currently, the most common synthesis procedures of DES include heating and stirring, ultrasonication, and vacuum evaporation [19]. Yet, these procedures need complicated instruments and consume heating and energy for long periods. To overcome this problem, a nonconventional and eco-friendly microwave treatment was employed for DES preparation within 60 sec for the first time. Thus, dual microwave treatment enables large-scale production of CDs and DES with reliable quality, holding significant importance.

Recently, CDs have gained attention in trace drug analysis owing to their perfect photostability, favorable biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, low cost, customizable surface chemistry, exceptional selectivity and sensitivity for analytes, and high quantum yield (Ф) [20]. They have been extensively used in the detection of pharmaceutical compounds [18,21–24], metal ions, biological imaging, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalytic degradation [24]. Yet, the global call for circular economy practices and green synthesis of luminescent biocompatible CDs from waste has been the hot topic on the surface. By looking at humans’ pharmaceutical usage, which is worth up to a million tons every year, this inspired us to upcycle the expired medicine wastes. The disposal of the expired pharmaceuticals remains a challenge as they may be dumped into the environment, causing health risks to the human beings [25]. Thus, it is an economical way to employ such medicines to fabricate novel CDs. The “Waste-to-Wealth” strategy is considered for the management of expired medications as well as the possibility of finding new precursors for the creation of value-added CDs. Among these medicines is the anticonvulsant gabapentin (GABA), which was selected as an excellent starting feedstock because of its prevalent use, richness of carbon, and its amino acid-like structure. The suitable choice of expired ammonium persulfate (AP) as a strong oxidizing agent and polymerization initiator boosts the CDs’ formation. Also, AP acts as a good nitrogen and sulfur dopant. So, in this study, we realized the large-scale formation of gabapentin-derived CDs (GABA-CDs) with deep blue color emission from expired GABA and AP for the first time. The native fluorescence of GABA-CDs is substantially quenched by FLV, permitting its sensitive determination. The variables affecting its spectrofluorimetric determination have been studied by design of experiments (DOE) to get comprehensive information on the critical parameters, explore the interactions between variables, and save effort, reagents, and time [26].

On the other hand, the urge to determine analytes in biological fluids with solvent-minimized techniques has drawn attention to liquid–liquid microextraction (LLME). Compared to other techniques, LLME has many benefits, including good recovery, shorter extraction times, less extractant volume, simplicity of use, and reproducibility. Herein, an air-assisted LLME (AA-LLME) was optimized for FLV extraction from plasma using a novel DES. The DES was prepared utilizing cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA), and glycerol as a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) via MWAS within 60 sec. As a flavonoid derivative with low water solubility, it has been proven that hydrophobic DES would be an ideal extractant for FLV [27]. However, no attempts have been made till now to use DES for FLV analysis in plasma. The synergism of microwave-aided DES preparation and air bubbles-aided solvent extraction creates a green strategy for selective FLV extraction using a greener, more sustainable, biodegradable, and cheap solvent [28]. The main benefit of this technique is the formation of extractant's fine droplets, which greatly enlarges the contact area between the two immiscible liquids in a short period without additional need for equipment or reagents [29].

Accordingly, for the first time, an air-aided DES-LLME (AA-DES-LLME) was introduced for the extraction of FLV from human plasma prior to its analysis via GABA-CDs fluorescence quenching. The optimization of the whole extraction step was studied in detail. The proposed method capitalizes on factorial design-optimized nanoprobe and AA-DES-LLME for FLV determination for the first time. Dual benefiting from MWAS in the fabrication of both GABA-CDs and DES greatly saves energy, time, and money and preserves the environment. So, this study offers accurate, sensitive, and quick outcomes, taking a few minutes, proving to be highly beneficial when time is of essence in applications and could contribute substantially to the FLV analytical profile. Significantly, this investigation paves the way for more applications of MWAS in DES preparation since this research area has not been explored yet. Additionally, greenness and blueness assessment tools were employed effectively, involving ComplexGAPI [30], Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) [31], and Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) [32].

The fluorescence spectra records were conducted using an Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectrofluorimeter (USA) with a 150 W xenon flash lamp, grating excitation and emission monochromators, slit widths of 5 nm, and samples were quantified in a one cm quartz cell. Spectra were smoothed with a factor of 20. Also, Shimadzu UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Kyoto-Japan), dual beam 1601 was employed in this study. The synthesis of GABA-CDs and DES was carried out via domestic microwave, WANSA oven, WP800P28-2 of 800 W. For characterization of DES, 1H NMR was performed via a Bruker 400 MHz Avance III spectrometer (Switzerland), and the chemical shifts were scanned at ppm. A Sigma 2-16P universal laboratory centrifuge from Sigma Laborzentrifugen GmbH-Germany and a vortex IVM 300p from Taiwan were utilized. An ultrasonic bath SS 101H 230 was used. The pH measurements were performed by a Consort pH meter, P-901 from Belgium. Full factorial design was utilized to optimize the analysis parameters using Minitab® Software (release 16, State College, USA).

A pure FLV sample (99.0 % purity) was obtained from Amoun Pharmaceutical Co., Cairo, Egypt. Nephroflam® tablets (batch no. 120977) product of Unipharma (Al-Obour city near Cairo, Egypt) labeled to contain 200 mg of FLV per tablet were purchased from the local pharmacy. GABA with expiry date: February 2011 and AP (Universal Laboratories U.F.C, Belami Fine Chemicals Pvt., India) with expiration date: December 2016 were collected from our laboratory. Sodium hydroxide and acids like phosphoric (98.0 %), acetic (99.7 %), and boric acids were supplied from El-Nasr Chemical Co., Cairo, Egypt. CTAB and glycerol (99.0 % assay) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA) and PioChem (Sixth of October City, Egypt), respectively. 0.45 µm membrane filters from Millipore, Ireland and human lung fibroblast (WI38 Cells) cell line from (VACSERA, Cairo, Egypt) were used. Plasma was acquired from Mansoura University Hospital, Egypt, and was kept frozen at −20 °C before analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard solution and buffer
ce:section.ce:para: In a 50-mL volumetric flask, FLV stock solution of 200 µg mL−1 was freshly prepared by dissolving 10 mg in methanol [1]. The same solvent was used for dilution to prepare the working solution. The solutions were wrapped in aluminum foil [1].

Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) covering the pH range of 2.0–12.0 was prepared by mixing a solution of 0.04 M of each of boric, phosphoric, and acetic acids, where the pH was adjusted using the necessary volumes of 0.2 M sodium hydroxide.

A 1.026 g of expired GABA and 1.368 g of AP waste were dissolved in 15 mL of water in a 50 mL beaker with sonication for 5 min. The solution was placed in a household microwave oven for 3 min. After cooling, 20 mL of water was added to the resulting residue and kept in the sonicator for 30 min, followed by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min and filtration. Subsequently, the resulting clear supernatant was kept in an amber-colored bottle in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further applications and was stable for six months. Upon analysis, 10 mL of this yellow solution was diluted to a final volume of 100 mL with water, to prepare a working solution.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100 JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was used to determine the morphology and size of the sample. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired on a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer, Thermo Scientific, USA. Scanning electron microscopy and Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis for morphological examination and elemental composition determination were conducted using SEM-EDX Jeol-jsm-6510LV, Tokyo, Japan. Also, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was conducted, utilizing monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation, Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Zeta potential of GABA-CDs was determined using a Zetasizer, Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK.

The WI38 cell line was seeded at 1.0 × 104 cells per well in a 96-well plate and cultivated for the entire night at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum, penicillin (100 units mL−1), and streptomycin (100 units mL−1). The cells were treated with GABA-CDs (2–100 µg mL−1) in 96-wells and subsequently incubated for 48 h. After that, 20 µL of methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution in phosphate buffer saline (5 mg mL−1) was added to the cells in each hole, which were incubated for further 4 h to produce violet formazan. The crystals obtained on each well were dissolved with 100 µL DMSO. The absorbance of the resulting mixture was scanned at 570 nm via Biotek microplate reader, Gen5™, USA.

CTAB was mixed with glycerol in a molar ratio of 1:4 in a 250 mL beaker. Afterwards, the beaker was placed in the microwave oven for 60 sec to get a homogenous transparent liquid, signifying the successful synthesis of DES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classical thermal-assisted preparation of hydrophobic DES
ce:section.ce:para: The above-mentioned mixture under section “2.7.” in the same ratio was prepared by heating at 60 ○C under magnetic stirring until the transparent liquid was formed after 20 min.

A 750 µL of GABA-CDs was mixed with 1200 µL of BRB of pH 7.4 in a set of 10-mL calibrated flasks, followed by adding different volumes of FLV standard solution to produce the final concentrations (0.3–24.0 µg mL−1). The flasks were completed to 10 mL with water and mixed well. The fluorescence intensities (F) were recorded at 475 nm after excitation at 320 nm. Concurrently, the blank experiment was conducted, and its fluorescence intensity (F○) was recorded. The calibration graph was achieved by graphing Ln (F○/F) versus the final FLV concentrations in µg mL−1 and the regression equation was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FLV in Nephroflam® tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets were accurately weighed and finely grinded. An amount of this powder equivalent to 10 mg of FLV was transferred to a 50-mL calibrated flask with 80 mL of methanol and sonicated for 30 min to extract the drug. The volume was completed with the same solvent and filtered. The clear solution obtained was protected from light by wrapping it with aluminum foil. Proper volumes of the filtrate were transmitted into 10-mL calibrated flasks, and the analysis procedure was adopted as cited under 2.9.1. According to the previous regression equation, the concentrations of FLV in the tablets and % recoveries were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for AA-LLME of FLV from plasma based on hydrophobic DES
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of 500 µL of human plasma samples were added into a set of falcon tubes (15 mL) and spiked with FLV standard solution to achieve the final concentrations of (0.5–4.0 µg mL−1). Subsequently, 600 µL ofthe prepared DES and 6 % (w/v) of NaCl were added. The mixture was pulled into a syringe and pushed back six times to boost the mass transfer of the analyte to the DES phase. The cloudy solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to separate the two phases. Finally, the floated DES phase containing the extracted FLV was collected in a 10-mL volumetric flask, and the procedure under 2.9.1. was adopted.

The microextraction process was optimized by assessing five factors on the extraction of the analyte chronologically: different molar ratios of the DES precursors, microwave irradiation time, volume of DES added, number of air cycles, and salt concentration. The first two factors involved in the preparation procedure of DES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Upcycling of expired pharmaceuticals via MWAS of GABA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In situ microwave irradiation technology was employed for effective fabrication of water-soluble GABA-CDs. When compared to traditional synthetic methods, using green technology for CDs synthesis gives more exceptional benefits involving lower production costs, saving energy, and lower waste generation. Concepts of recycling and upcycling of different wastes into valued products have recently attracted a lot of interest in solving environmental concerns while supporting the circular economy concept. Because of the huge number of outdated medications that amasses annually worldwide, and their improper disposal poses an environmental risk, this inspired us to valorize these wastes into highly fluorescent nanoprobe. Among these expired medications is GABA. Being an amino acid analogue, this has driven our attention to utilize outdated GABA as a novel carbon source for CDs synthesis. The synthetic conditions were carefully tuned in detail, involving initiator type, ratios of the precursors, and microwave heating time.

Primarily, GABA alone was used as a starting material for CDs preparation, but fluorescence scanning indicates unsuccessful CDs formation (Fig. 1 a). Thus, for the sake of synthesis initiation, co-reactants like sodium phosphate, ammonium molybdate, potassium dichromate, and ferric aluminum sulfate were blended with GABA, but they also failed to generate CDs. Thus, AP was investigated to initiate CDs synthesis since it is known as polymerization initiator and oxidizing agent. Intriguingly, a mixture of AP with GABA showed dramatic enhancement of the Ф of CDs in a short heating period, as observed in Fig. 1a. Interestingly, AP plays a dual role in the formation of GABA-CDs: first, as a strong oxidizing agent and polymerization initiator [33] and second, as a rich dopant of heteroatoms. AP works through a free radical mechanism that can be explained based on the persulfate-dependent advanced oxidation process (AOP). Persulfate-dependent AOP involves in situ formed sulfate radical (SO4 •–) and hydroxyl radical (Fig. 1b). A large amount of sulfate radicals is generated from cleaving of the peroxide bond in the persulfate molecule by the influence of microwave energy. Further reaction of SO4 •– with water generates hydroxyl radical (OH•) [34]. These radicals induced the formation of GABA radicals and further free radical polymerization and aromatization leading to CDs formation. In addition, AP offers sulfur and nitrogen atoms. These heteroatoms could lessen the surface defect states and trap areas, which are advantageous in enhancing fluorescence intensity (FI). In parallel, the inserted heteroatoms could offer a wealth of functional groups or active sites to enhance the affinity of doping CDs to target analyte [35]. Besides, AP has some benefits, such as being economical, not potentially explosive, low toxicity, and affordability [36]. So, the expired GABA and AP blend was chosen for further study.

To optimize the GABA-CDs synthesis, various molar ratios of GABA and AP were investigated, involving 4:8, 6:6, 6:7, 6:8, 8:4, and 10:3 (mmol: mmol). Among these, the amount of 6 mmol of both constituents achieved the highest CDs’ Ф (Fig. 2 a). It was noticed that the ratios of 4:8 (GABA: AP) lowered Ф due to smaller proportion of the main carbon source (GABA). The Ф also lowered with the ratio of 8:4 and 10:3 (GABA: AP), due to incomplete conversion of GABA to CDs because of smaller proportion of AP that initiates the synthesis process. Nevertheless, a slight rise in the amount of AP above the optimum to 7 and 8 mmol caused a slight reduction in Ф. The reason behind this may be that high AP amounts raise the rate of the molecular chain termination reaction and perhaps result in the evanescence of active chain activity [37].

Owing to the important role of the microwave radiation time on CDs synthesis, several MWAS times were examined from 2 to 9 min. The results indicated that 3 min of heating gave the highest Ф (Fig. 2b). A subsequent decline happened when the time was further increased. This was due to the possibility that a longer microwave radiation duration would cause CDs to become more graphitized and produce large-sized CDs with lower photoluminescence [38]. During the first 1.5 min in the microwave, the solution is transparent. After that time, AP starts to react and initiate CDs formation. Next, carbon nuclei of CDs were generated [39], and the solution turns dark brown. Carbon nuclei subsequently create small-sized nanoparticles, GABA-CDs. Consequently, the carbonaceous expired GABA underwent a series of processes under microwave heating, including dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization, followed by in-situ surface passivation under optimal synthesis conditions.

The TEM picture (Fig. 3 a) showsthat GABA-CDs are well-dispersed with average particle size of 3.6 ± 0.62 nm (Fig. 3a inset). The surface functional groups were ascertained by examining the FTIR spectrum. The spectrum reveals –OH or N-H stretching, a C-H peak, and C-N peak at 3195, 2784, and 2100 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3b). Also, the peak at 1642 cm−1 is indicative of C=O, C=N or C=C groups and the peak at 1413 cm−1 indicates COO– vibration. The peaks at 1159, 1029, 856, and 576 cm−1 are assigned to C=S, S=O, C-H, and S-S, correspondingly [40]. The spectrum shows hydrophilic surface groups which support the excellent water solubility of GABA-CDs. Moreover, EDX was employed to investigate the elemental composition of the as-obtained GABA-CDs (Fig. 3c). Simultaneously, elemental mapping (Fig. 3d) shows the surface distribution of these elements.

The XPS analysis has been employed to ascertain the composition of GABA-CDs. The survey scanned XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e) exhibited carbon (C1s peak at 288.19 eV, 60.90 %), oxygen (O1s at 535.3 eV, 26.50 %), nitrogen (N1s peak at 404.21 eV, 8.06 %), and sulfur (S2p at 171.78 eV, 4.53 %), implying a successful and high level of doping. The C1s high resolution spectrum (Fig. S1a) shows three peaks at 284.73, 284.97, and 288.34 eV corresponding to CC, C–C/C–H, and O–CO/N–CO, respectively. The O1s high resolution spectrum (Fig. S1b) revealed two peaks at 533.31 and 534.13 eV, corresponding to C–O and O–CO, respectively. High resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) shows two peaks; CN (at 398.82 eV) and (CO)–N–(CO) (at 400.69 eV) [41]. The S2p peak deconvoluted into five bands at 163.24, 164.49, 172.87, 175.39, and 177.79 eV (Fig. S1d), corresponding to S–S, R–S(O)(O)–R′, SO4 2−, and HSO4 −, respectively [42]. The zeta potential value (ζ) of –33.54 mV had resulted from the aqueous solution of GABA-CDs (Fig. 3f), indicating the presence of highly dense negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on their surface. This explains the stability of GABA-CDs due to their repulsion.
So, the prepared nanoparticles exhibit narrow size and hydrophilic polar moieties (–NH, –COO-, S=O, and –OH).

The typical UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GABA-CDs is displayed in Fig. 4 a. It showed a sharp peak at 211 nm, which might be assigned to π-π∗ transitions of aromatic CC. While the absorption peak at 300 nm could be attributed to the n-π∗ transition of CO and CN on the surface.

Interestingly, the emission spectra of GABA-CDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 300to 420 nm. Fig. 4b displayed characteristic excitation-independent emission. This behavior is attributed to the uniform tiny particle size of the furnished GABA-CDs [43]. There is an enhancement in FI upon excitation at wavelengths from 300 to 370 nm. Upon excitation at 370 nm, the strongest emission occurred in the blue regions at 475 nm (quantum yield of 16.5 %). A large Stokes’ shift of 105 nm between the excitation and emission of GABA-CDs suggested a huge promise for analytical applications. Owing to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups like −CO, −O–H, −C–O, and electro-donating groups as –NH2, the designed GABA-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence with high Ф. In addition, the surface states of nanoparticle carbon materials and electron-hole recombination contribute to the fluorescence property [44]. Furthermore, it is stated that the insertion of sulfur atoms in the form of CS to CDs’ surface could be responsible for the high Ф [44]. Nitrogen doping is advantageous in forming a conjugated π electron system, hinting that the fluorescence mechanism is mostly dependent on hybridization between the molecular state and carbon backbone [45]. From the viewed features of the designed GABA-CDs, it could be proclaimed that they comprise of optically sensitive defect points, which render them a promising material for high performance applications, particularly in the areas of chemical sensing and biomedical applications.

In order to study the stability of the as-obtained GABA-CDs, the effect of various pH media, time, temperature, and UV–Vis irradiation was tested by monitoring FI. As a fundamental feature of the external solution environment, pH played a critical role in FI of GABA-CDs. The pH effect was studied over the range of 2.0–12.0 using universal BRB. The FI was constant in the pH range from 2.0 to 5.0, while it showed some increase (17 %) as the pH rose from 6.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 4c). This pH-dependent behavior was most likely caused by the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2) on the surface of GABA-CDs [46]. From pH 6.0 to 12.0, the surface carboxylic groups tended to be in ionized forms (–COO−), which strengthened the electrostatic repulsion between the particles and raised the FI.

The effect of time on the stability of the GABA-CDs was explored by monitoring their fluorescence for up to one hour (Fig. S2). The findings indicated that the FI stayed constant, signifying excellent stability. Moreover, thermal stability was examined over a broad temperature range from 15 to 100 ○C. As observed in Fig. 4d, no remarkable decline in FI across the tested temperatures, indicating that heating/cooling has a negligible effect on GABA-CDs’ stability. This may be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on GABA-CDs’ surface, which can insulate the fluorescence centers and counteract degradation from heating by stabilizing particles’ aggregation [47].

Moreover, photostability is pivotal for real-world sensing applications, which necessitates stability in ambient environments. Upon continuous UV illumination at 365 nm, there was no noticeable decrease in FI of GABA-CDs after 30 min, as well as only a 3 % decline after 60 min, signifying the excellent photostability. Also, the probe showed only an 8 % decline in FI after being stored at 4 °C for six months with no precipitation or aggregation. The stability of GABA-CDs under harsh environmental conditions ensures robust nanoparticles and fluorimetric analysis.

To validate the safety and biocompatibility of the GABA-CDs, the conventional MTT assay was conducted to determine the cytotoxicity towards WI38 cell line. The results indicated that following 48 h of incubation, the WI38 viability was high even after the addition of 100 µg mL−1 (Fig. S3). The IC50 value of the GABA-CDs was found to be 183.13 µg mL−1 in comparison to 6.72 µg mL−1 of doxorubicin as the reference. It implies that GABA-CDs have minimal toxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent nanoprobe for FLV detection and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The stability, excellent fluorescence property, and biocompatibility of the designed GABA-CDs make them reliable for building a fluorescence sensing platform for FLV. Examination of the fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of GABA-CDs and the absorption spectrum of FLV (Fig. 4a) inspired the idea of tailoring the GABA-CDs fluorescence by using λex/λem of 320/475 nm. Since FLV exhibits maximum absorption at 320 nm, maximum sensitivity could be achieved by excitating GABA-CDs at 320 nm where their fluorescence is quantitively quenched by the inner filtration effect (IFE) of FLV. This approach is corroborated by the excitation-independent emission of GABA-CDs that leads to emission peak centered at 475 nm regardless of λex. It is evident that FLV substantially quenches GABA-CDs fluorescence in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 4e).

To further ensure the IFE-induced quenching mechanism, the observed fluorescence intensity (Fobs) was corrected by subtracting the IFE via the general Eq. (1) [48]:
(1)
F
corr
=
F
obs
×
antilog
(
A
ex
+
A
em
2
)
where, Fcorr refers to the corrected FI if IFE is excluded, Aex and Aem denote the absorbance at the optimal λex and λem of GABA-CDs.

As shown in Fig. S4a, the IFE was removed to produce the suppressed efficiency (%E) for the corrected and observed fluorescence of GABA-CDs according to Eq. (2) [48]:
(2)
% E =
1 - F /
F
0
× 100
It was found that approximately 49 % of %E arises from IFE. So, there is another mechanism coexisting in the remaining 51 % of %E.

To figure out the other mechanism, the Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) was applied at three different temperatures (313, 323, 333 K) [49].
(3)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
F L V
where the Stern-Volmer constant is symbolized as Ksv. In dynamic quenching, an excited fluorescence molecule collides with the quencher, leading to non-radiative loss of energy, whereas in static quenching, the complex is formed by combining the ground state fluorescence molecule with the quencher [49]. Fig. S4b displays the plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) against FLV concentrations. The slope values (Ksv) relate inversely with temperature, hinting a static quenching mechanism. As a result of diminished complex stability caused by higher temperatures, a fall in Ksv is expected at higher temperatures. The absorption peaks with and without FLV (quencher) were also recorded to further verify static quenching. As can be observed from Fig. S4c, the absorption peaks changed, signifying a new compound was formed. Briefly, the quenching of fluorescence with the addition of FLV was induced by the synergistic effect of primary IFE and static quenching mechanisms.

Looking at FLV structure (Fig. 4f), it demonstrates that it can create various interactions which facilitate the affinity of GABA-CDs to FLV. FLV contains carbonyl moieties which can be involved in hydrogen bonding with –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 groups of the probe. Concurrently, the strong electron withdrawing nature of conjugated ketone moiety in FLV creates electron deficient attached π-skeleton. Thus, this part acts as an electron acceptor to interact with the polarized aromatic electron rich core (electron donors) of GABA-CDs [50]. Thereby, the specific π-π electron donor acceptor interactions between FLV and GABA-CDs is expected. Additionally, π-π stacking interactions between aromatic moiety of FLV and the aromatic core of GABA-CDs bestowed a chemical driving force for the high affinity to FLV (Fig. 4f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for FLV sensing by GABA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the optimization of experimental conditions directly impacts the sensor's performance, it is evident that it is one of the most crucial steps in sensor creation. In this study, the optimal conditions for FLV sensing were achieved by applying full factorial design. This has numerous merits over the traditional method of one factor at a time, including the ability to identify the critical factors and interactions between them with fewer experimental trials [26,51]. The screening step showed three key variables, involving BRB pH, the volume of BRB, and the volume of GABA-CDs. These preliminary experiments were conducted as follows to identify the level for each variable carefully.

Primarily, the impact of pH on F0/F was studied. It was found that F0/F values enhanced in the pH range from 7.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S5a). Taking into consideration that FLV is unstable in acidic and alkaline media [4], along with the goal of analyzing it in plasma, the selected pH range was 7.0 to 7.4 for factorial design. Accordingly, the effect of BRB volume on F0/F was studied by using several volumes from 500 to 3000 µL. It was noticed that F0/F increased gradually with rising volumes up to 1200 µL and remained constant until 1500 µL; after that, F0/F values declined (Fig. S5b). So, the volumes of BRB were chosen from 1200 to 1500 µL for factorial design. Afterwards, the effect of GABA-CDs volume on F0/F was examined. Upon raising the volume from 300 to 750 µL, F0/F increased substantially and then remained relatively stable with further increasing volume (Fig. S5c). Therefore, the range from 750 to 1000 µL was selected.

Finally, the impact of incubation time on F0/F was examined. As observed in Fig. S2, the reaction between FLV and GABA-CDs happens spontaneously and stays stable for more than one hour, which adds another advantage to the methodology.

Next, a 23 full factorial design was applied as it included two levels and three independent factors. Minitab® software suggests a set of eight experiments after inserting the lowest and highest levels for each factor (Table S1). After performing these experiments, the resulting response (F0/F) for each was entered into the program. The response optimizer in the Minitab® program determined the optimal composite (D) and individual (d) desirability values. These values range from zero to one and evaluate the optimization process. When these values are close to one, this indicates optimal conditions and achievement of the goal [51].
From the optimization plot (Fig. S6) and response optimizer, it was manifested that pH 7.4, BRB volume of 1200 µL, and GABA-CDs volume of 750 µL are the optimal experimental conditions (Table S2).

According to the Pareto chart (Fig. S7a), main effects plot (Fig. S7b), and normal plot (Fig. S7c), the buffer volume has the highest negative impact on F0/F, but it is not significant at a 95 % confidence level. From interaction plots (Fig. S7d), the buffer volume has a negative impact when it interacts with pH both at high and low levels. In addition, the interaction between BRB volume and GABA-CDs volume affects positively F0/F at high level, while at low level there is no noticeable effect.

The proposed method was validated according to ICH guidelines [52]. Such a unique quenching feature of the as-prepared GABA-CDs to FLV aided in the development of sensing applications. Fig. 4e displays the turn-off of FI of GABA-CDs upon the addition of FLV with a concentration range of 0.3–24.0 µg mL−1. So, a linear relationship was established between this FLV concentration range and Ln (F0/F) (Fig. S8), and the fitted linear equation as follows:
Ln (F0/F) = 0.041C + 0.026 (r = 0.9999)

The limits of detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) could be estimated to be 0.094 and 0.285 µg mL−1, respectively (Table S3) by the ICH recommended equations Eq. (4) and (5) [52]:
(4)
LOD = 3 . 3
σ / b
(5)
LOQ = 10
σ / b
where
σ
is the standard deviation of y-intercept of regression equation and b is the slope of line. The analytical findings (Table S3) establish the feasibility of GABA-CDs as FLV nanoprobe.

Testing the accuracy of the designed approach was performed through analyzing different concentrations of standard FLV solutions. Good percentage recoveries (99.13–102.20 %) were attained, demonstrating the method's good accuracy (Table 1 ). Furthermore, the results of the studied approach were compared with those of the official BP spectrophotometric procedure [1] using Student’s t-test and variance ratio F-test [53]. The BP method [1] is based on measuring the absorbance at 293 nm using 0.1 M HCl as the diluting solvent. The calculated t and F values were found to be less than the tabulated ones, confirming that there is no significant difference between the two approaches (Table 1), verifying the method’s accuracy.

The precision of the approach was validated by testing intraday and inter-day levels, where %RSD was measured in both cases. Intraday precision was performed by determining three different FLV concentrations on the same day, while inter-day precision was evaluated by determining the same concentrations on three consecutive days. All %RSD values were ≤ 1.88% (Table S4), demonstrating the method’s high precision.

The robustness was verified via adopting minor modifications in the examined experimental parameters, involving the pH of the BRB (7.4 ± 0.1), BRB volume (1200 ± 100 µL), and GABA-CDs volume (750 ± 100 µL). The results in Table S5 demonstrate that no remarkable change was detected in the analytical performance of FLV, as SD values ranged from 1.18 to 1.99 in all cases.

The synthesized GABA-CDs were employed as a fluorescent probe to detect FLV in biological samples and pharmaceuticals. Prior to determining their feasibility, it was necessary to ensure their high selectivity. In this vein, the effect of co-administered drugs on the sensor’s fluorescence was evaluated, such as paracetamol, levodopa, ascorbic acid, spiramycin, aceclofenac, dexamethasone, and prednisolone. To further evaluate the selectivity, the influence of various potential interfering materials and metal ions was studied, such as lactose, glucose, sucrose, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose, citric acid, sodium citrate, starch, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, HCO3 –, S2O3 2−, and SO4 2−. Also, amino acids like alanine, arginine, and lysine have been investigated. The obtained %change of fluorescence values were in the range from −0.13 to + 6.65 % (Table 2 ), showing the high selectivity and anti-interference capability of GABA-CDs for FLV detection in complex matrices.

The proposed method was used for the analysis of FLV in pharmaceutical tablets to explore its feasibility for real-world applications. The results revealed that the recoveries of FLV in Nephroflam® tablets ranged from 98.97 % to 101.43 % with %RSD of 1.02 % (Table 1). Also, the tablets were analyzed by the official spectrophotometric procedure [1] to statistically compare the results of the two methods using t and F tests [53]. The calculated t and F values (Table 1) prove that there is no significant difference between the analytical performance of the studied approach and the official one [1] with regard to accuracy and precision, respectively. Consequently, the approach’s sensitivity, selectivity, accuracy, and precision certify its recommendation for quality control units in pharmaceutical analysis.

Fast analysis of FLV in plasma is valuable in medication and clinical testing. The complexity of plasma samples can pose challenges for the fluorescent-based sensing platform due to the interfering endogenous substances that often impact the target analyte detection, necessitating the removal of the interferents beforehand. Integrating an approach that can cope with such complexities is vital to guarantee the reliability and accuracy of the sensing platform in real-world cases.

The maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of FLV is 0.7 µg mL−1 [54]. This concentration lies within the linearity range of the studied approach (0.3–24.0 µg mL−1), signifying its applicability for accurate analysis of FLV in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between green DES microwave treatment and traditional heating and stirring techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The constant pursuit of environmentally friendly chemistry has brought in the creation of innovative materials that fulfil green chemistry's principles. This motivates us to conduct DES preparation in microwave radiation. The microwave takes only one minute compared to all previously published DES heating and stirring procedures that consume several hours. So, microwave treatment in this study is considered the fastest technique. It also provides efficient energy transmission to the system [55] with no additional equipment needed. Finally, MWAS greatly streamlines the entire procedure and is the first step towards achieving DES on a larger scale.
The as-produced DES from CTAB and glycerol is water-immiscible. The DES and its initial feedstocks were characterized using FTIR and 1H NMR spectra to analyze and compare their chemical structure.

The key aspect behind the formation of DES is the hydrogen bonding between CTAB and glycerol upon heating. In this respect, FTIR was used to study the interactions of functional groups in the formed DES and its reactants. The broad absorption band at 3370 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CTAB (Fig. S9a) signifies the −NH4 + moiety, in addition to stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2 groups at 2848 and 2914 cm−1. The peaks at 1472 and 1422 cm−1 are ascribed to the symmetric and asymmetric vibration of N+–CH, while the peak at 960 cm−1 belongs to the out-of-plane –CH vibration of –CH3 [56]. The peak at 722 cm−1 could be ascribed to Br−. Regarding the FTIR spectrum of glycerol, the broad band at 3281 cm−1 is ascribed to O-H group stretching and bending, while the characteristic bands at 2915 and 2878 cm−1 are related to C-H bonds [57]. The peaks at 1415 and 1031 cm−1 are related to the C-O moiety while the peak at 702 cm−1 refers to CH2 moiety. In the spectrum of DES, the vibration frequency of the O-H moiety moved in the direction of higher frequency from 3281 cm−1 in glycerol to 3303 cm−1, which indicated that an intermolecular hydrogen bond of O–H⋯Brwas formed. This shift was ascribed to the oxygen electrons cloud point moving to the hydrogen bonds upon inclusion of CTAB, which lowers the constant force and modifies the vibrational state [58]. Another evidence of the interaction is the shift of CTAB peak (2848 cm−1) and glycerol peak (2878 cm−1) to 2885 cm−1. The peak at 722 cm−1 in the DES spectrum originated from Br− of CTAB, which further signified the successful incorporation of the two precursors.

Similarly, 1H NMR spectra of HBD, HBA, and DES were determined using DMSO as solvent to establish that the produced DES was free from impurities and no chemical interactions present (Fig. S9b). The peaks at 3.01 and 1.21 ppm in DES originate from CTAB incorporation. The peaks of active hydrogen nuclei (O–H) exhibit downfield shift (δ = 4.511 ppm) in contrast with free glycerol (δ = 4.410 ppm), which was mainly brought on by the deshielding impact of the hydrogen bonds and the decline in the electron cloud point [59]. Additionally, no extra peaks arose in the DES compared to the precursors, so no new chemical bonds were generated during its formation; rather, it was a physical proceeding.

The hydrophobicity of the interested analyte, defined as log P, indicates the extraction efficiency using LLME. FLV contains hydrophobic aromatic moieties and hydrophilic ester and carbonyl groups (Fig. 4e), but the hydrophobic nature predominates, as implied by the log P value of 4.9 [60]. This motivates us to perform LLME of FLV from plasma samples. As a new class of ecologically friendly solvents, DESs were investigated as extractive media in LLME since they were safe, cheap, easily prepared, highly selective, and readily accessible. DESs employed for extraction must fulfil several criteria, involving a high affinity for specific analytes, a liquid state at room temperature, no interference with the analytical signal, a density distinct from the matrix medium (e.g., water), and high stability [61]. The designed extraction workflow is schematically presented in Fig. 5a.

So far, DESs are synthesized via either heating, ultrasonication and heating, grinding, or vacuum evaporation [19]. In this study, we designed a novel, ultrafast, and highly efficient DES synthesis approach via MWAS of a mixture of glycerol and CTAB for 60 sec at 800 W. Furthermore, MWAS was compared with the conventional stirring and heating procedure. Primarily, the latter method was conducted at 60 °C, taking 20 min for DES formation. The typical process for the synthesis of DES was presented in detail in Fig. S10. Various experimental variables that impact the extraction efficiency of FLV by AA-DES-LLME have been investigated to achieve the optimal microextraction conditions, as displayed in Fig. 5 b–f. These variables, from DES synthesis to the LLME process, involve molar ratio of constituents of DES, microwave heating time, DES volume, air cycles number, and salt concentration.

The extraction capacity of the procedure depends on diverse interactions like electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between DES and FLV. Thus, four different DES types were studied using CTAB and glycerol at 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, and 1:6 M ratios. The findings demonstrated that F0/F values increased as the glycerol molar ratio rose up to 1:4 (Fig. 5b), which was applied. These findings may be because glycerol partially supplies hydrophobic properties to the DES solution. As the glycerol molar ratio rises from 2 to 4, it leads to carbon chain, boosting the microextraction efficiency in LLME. Further increase in glycerol molar ratio to 6 would enhance the viscosity of the DES-water solution greatly, impeding FLV transfer into the DES phase. The prepared DES incorporates both hydrophilic (–OH and −NH4 +) and hydrophobic (long carbon chain) regions. The –OH groups of glycerol can interact with the basic nitrogen atom/carbonyl group in FLV via hydrogen bonding. This offers a hydrophilic interaction site that boosts the solubility of the polar areas of the medication.

The microwave irradiation time was further examined in the range of 0.5–3 min. As shown in Fig. 5c, the extraction efficiency was optimal when the irradiation time was one min. As high temperature for a longer time may potentially degrade DES or its constituents prior to producing DES, which may be linked to the generation of hot spots [19]. Microwave heating arises from the capacity of the reactants (CTAB and glycerol) to absorb the energy of microwave irradiation and transform it into thermal energy. This can happen via two mechanisms: (i) ionic conduction and (ii) the rotation of dipole molecules [62]. Since the ionic conductivity in the case of DESs is restricted, heating via the rotation of dipole molecules is necessary. The molecules must have the ability to move in order for the phenomena to occur, and as a result, they must align themselves in accordance with the direction and recurrence of the alternating electromagnetic field [55]. During rotation, the microwave irradiation energy is transformed into kinetic energy and imparted to each other by colliding of HBA with HBD compounds. Consequently, the substance (DES’s components) receives a uniform distribution of heat.

During the AA-DES-LLME process, the volume of DES is a vital factor that affects the extraction performance and method’s sensitivity via modifying the quality of the generated cloudy state. Fig. 5d illustrates that F0/F increased gradually from 200 to 500 µL and then remained constant until 700 µL, followed by decreasing again due to the dilution effect. So, 600 µL of DES was chosen for further experiments.

In AA-DES-LLME, it is expected that the solutes’ extraction efficiency is improved by raising the number of air cycles owing to the improved contact area between the sample and extractant tremendously [63]. Moreover, air bubbles could aid in breaking down emulsions that may form during the extraction procedure. Emulsions can reduce the extraction efficiency and complicate the process of separating DES and aqueous phases. Therefore, the number of air cycles was executed in the range of 2–10 cycles. As seen in Fig. 5e, F0/F values of FLV enhanced up to six cycles and then gradually declined. A lower number of air cycles led to insufficient dispersion of DES, while substantially more cycles increased the analyte's water solubility and consequently diminished the extraction efficiency [63]. Hence, six air agitation cycles were selected for further study. It takes only 45 s for the dispersion, which adds an extra merit to the microextraction procedure in terms of rapidity.

Salt addition is one of the primary factors that can impact extraction efficiency by enhancing analyte transfer to the DES phase and reducing solubility in aqueous solution (salting-out action). On the other hand, it can alter the physical characteristics of Nernst diffusion film and reduce the diffusion rate of the target analytes into the DES phase (salting in action) [63,64]. In this study, the salt effect was tested by varying NaCl concentrations: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 w/v%. Fig. 5f presented that raising NaCl concentration up to 6 % led to an enhancing trend in F0/F, while higher concentrations negatively influenced F0/F owing to enhanced viscosity and density of the solution and, hence, hindering extraction [59]. Thus, the study continued with 6 % (w/v) of NaCl.

The optimized microextraction conditions showed excellent linear dependency between Ln (F○/F) and FLV concentrations (µg mL−1) (Table 3 ) with a significant recovery range of 91.0–104.5 % in plasma. In view of simplicity, fast analysis time, and low reagents and equipment cost, our approach combining the nanosensor and AA-DES-LLME could be feasible for monitoring FLV in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Double color-coded appraisal: greenness and blueness of the designed technique
ce:section.ce:para: Using multiple greenness evaluation tools achieves synergistic and harmonious results, facilitating a more thorough and informative comparison between analytical methods [65]. Herein, ComplexGAPI [30], AGREE [31], and BAGI [32] tools were introduced. ComplexGAPI evaluates the overall sustainability of an analytical process, from sample selection and preparation to the final assay, along with the pre-analysis [30]. The pre-analysis steps are symbolized by an additional hexagonal shape at the evaluation base. On a color scale, the environmental impact of this tool is displayed; red denotes an extremely dangerous impact, while yellow and green denote medium and low impacts, respectively [30]. The ComplexGAPI pictogram of the current study showed more green zones owing to the safe reagents and the omission of high temperature (Table S6). While the single red zone is ascribed to the off-line collection.

AGREE clockwise pictogram is automatically divided into twelve segments and the overall central score ranges from zero to one [31]. Interestingly, the studied method reveals an AGREE score of 0.7 with almost green zones (Table S6). While the other spectrofluorimetric procedure [16] has a score of 0.63 with more red shades. Zone 3 is red due to off-line analysis. Zone 10 is yellow in our study as some chemicals are biobased as glycerol while CTAB, GABA, and AP are not biobased. While in the other procedure, all reagents are not biobased, so the zone was red.

Recently released, the user-friendly BAGI evaluates analytical methods for functionality [32]. It is based on ten aspects involving technique, chemicals, instruments, and automation degree. In addition to sample-related parameters like amount, the kind of preparation step, and throughput. The results are displayed as an asteroid pictogram with a central score. Each section is colored with different blue color grades or even white, to point to high to low, or no compliance to the applicable characteristics, respectively [32]. Our procedure earned a solid BAGI score of 75, while the published spectrofluorimetric procedure [16] had a score of 67.5, signifying the cost-effectiveness, high throughput, and practicality of the proposed method (Table S6).

The cost efficiency of the studied method is obvious, as it relies on the upcycling and repurposing of cheap and expired substances, domestic microwave oven, and mix-and-measure procedure. In addition, it omits organic solvents. The one-pot synthesis process is completed in a very short time and consumes less energy compared to the documented methods for CDs and DES preparation. These criteria all boost the double color greenness and blueness of the developed technique and encourage its applicability for FLV assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the designed FLV sensing strategy and the previously published methods
ce:section.ce:para: Most of the reported methods for FLV determination are HPLC methods, which are costly especially when combined with mass spectrometric detector to achieve the plasma concentration (Cmax). HPLC requires highly specialized analysts and consumes large volumes of hazardous organic solvents. In addition, it is time-consuming, needing time for equilibration, column preconditioning, analysis, and washing steps. The electrochemical methods involve multistep, lack of reproducibility, and short life span of the electrodes, rendering them complex technique. Besides, these methods need multiple chemicals for electrode construction like graphite powder [12,13], tetraphenylborate, o-nitro phenyloctyl ether as plasticizer, phosphotungestic acid [12], paraffin oil, and ferrocene (dissolved in tetrabutylammoniumhexafluoro-phosphate, which was prepared in acetonitrile) [13]. Also, spectrophotometric methods are not sensitive enough.

On the other hand, the suggested approach is superior to the previous methods in ease of use, mix-and-read analysis, high throughput, and cheap and affordable instrumentation. The obvious benefit of this study is higher sensitivity over most reported methods [5,6,8,11–13,15], as implied from LOD values (Table 4 ). Finally, this study presented an innovative and sustainable C, N, and S source for GABA-CDs fabrication from expired GABA and AP waste. It could be recommended in routine quality control labs and pharmacokinetic studies, with good accuracy, precision, robustness, and reliability. Despite the sensitivity of the proposed method is less than some of the reported methods [7,9,10,16], yet these methods relied on LC-mass detector [10] or HPLC techniques [7,9] that necessitate expensive equipment, high experience to operate the instrument, and plenty of organic solvents for mobile phase preparation. Also, some of these methods [7,16] did not study real application in complex matrix like plasma, which limit their applicability. The feasibility of scaling up the microwave method for large-scale production of GABA-CDs and DES for extensive research or commercial applications might pose particular practical challenges, currently the subject of more studies in our laboratory.

AA-DES-LLME in this work is more favorable than other traditional extraction techniques that relied on protein precipitation [9,10,14]. These techniques required additional electric equipment, multistep, and 400 [9] and 1000 [10] µL of acetonitrile, which is an expensive and toxic organic solvent (Table S7). In contrast, the green DES successfully extracted FLV from plasma without adding ligands or emulsifier agents in only 45 s by air bubbling.

Fluorescent GABA-CDs combined with AA-DES-LLME was validated as an innovative, rapid, and sustainable analytical platform for feasible and sensitive detection of FLV. The GABA-CDs were engineered via one-pot microwave treatment from repurposing of a mixture of expired GABA and AP (6:6, mmol: mmol) as valueless wastes in only 3 min. Various characterization techniques confirmed the tiny GABA-CDs (3.6 ± 0.62 nm), co-doped with S and N, and exhibited plenty of polar function groups like O-H, COOH, and N-H. The intrinsic fluorescence of GABA-CDs was quenched by FLV owing to the joint action of IFE and static quenching. A DOE was conducted to optimize the sensing reliably and rapidly. Subsequently, the GABA-CDs are successfully adopted to determine FLV in tablets with good selectivity. The excellent biocompatibility and low LOD of 0.094 µg mL−1 permit the effective assay of FLV in human plasma with good percentage recoveries (91.0 %- 104.5 %). Thus, AA-DES-LLME was employed for extraction of FLV from plasma samples, omitting toxic organic extractants.The prepared DES was synergized from environmentally benign materials, CTAB and glycerol via 60 sec microwave treatment for the first time. The new DES is readily suitable for extraction of flavonoid derivatives from different matrices. This study opens a new avenue to boost and empower the uses of microwave radiation in analytical chemistry labs during different phases of methodology development, starting from reagent to extracting media synthesis. Additionally, the approach is easy to perform, accurate, and precise, which recommends its application in routine quality control and pharmacokinetics studies. The analysis aligns with circular economy practice and green chemistry as evidenced by ComplexGAPI, AGREE, and BAGI metrics.

Tolba: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
